1.2: havel havalim amar qohelet havel havalim hakol havel: 'Vanity of vanities, says the Preacher, vanity of vanities! All is vanity.' The Hebrew hevel (vapor, breath, vanity) is used 38 times in Ecclesiastes — more than in any other biblical book. It literally means a breath of air that passes immediately: something that exists momentarily and then is gone. The LXX translates hevel as mataiotes (futility, vanity), and Paul uses this word in Romans 8:20: 'the creation was subjected to futility [mataiotes].' The Ecclesiastes diagnosis is therefore not pessimism but realism about the post-fall condition of creation: all earthly striving that does not account for God and eternity is, sub specie aeternitatis, vapor. The NT's response is the resurrection, which gives permanence to what was formerly vapor: 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Cor 15:58).
1.1: Dibrei Qohelet ben-David melek biYerushalayim — 'The words of Qohelet, son of David, king in Jerusalem.' The title introduces the speaker by function rather than name: Qohelet (H6953) is a feminine participial noun from qahal (to assemble), meaning 'the one who assembles' or 'the preacher/assembler of an audience.' The feminine form is unusual for a title referring to a male king — it may function as an abstract office title (cf. Sophereth, Ezra 2:55). The Solomonic frame ('son of David, king in Jerusalem') is never made fully explicit by name; 'Qohelet' as speaker is maintained throughout. This allows the book to function as royal wisdom literature without being locked to Solomon's historical particulars.
1.3: Mah-yitron la'adam — 'What lasting gain does a person have?' The word yitron (H3504) is used ten times in Ecclesiastes and almost nowhere else in the OT (once in Exod 16:23 in an unrelated sense). It carries a commercial nuance: 'surplus,' 'profit,' 'net advantage after deducting costs.' The Teacher is asking whether there is a net positive return on all human investment. The prepositional phrase betahat hashemesh ('under the sun') appears 29 times in Ecclesiastes and is the book's self-imposed limit: it marks the sub-eschatological perspective, what can be known within the frame of mortal, earthly existence without resurrection or final judgment. The question of yitron is therefore precisely bounded: not 'is there value in life?' but 'is there net advantage within the closed system of existence under the sun?'
1.4: Dor holekh ve-dor ba ve-ha'aretz le'olam amedet — 'One generation goes and another generation comes, but the earth remains forever.' The contrast is structurally precise: human generations are characterized by movement — holekh (going) and ba (coming) — while the earth amedet (stands firm, endures). The verb amad for the earth's permanence contrasts with the transience of dor (generation). The phrase le'olam ('forever') here means 'continuously, without end' — it characterizes the earth's persistence, not its ultimate ontological status. The irony is that what appears most solid and permanent (the earth) outlasts what considers itself most significant (successive human generations).
1.5: Ve-zarach hashemesh uba hashemesh — 'The sun rises and the sun sets.' The root zarach (to rise/shine) is used for both sunrise and the dawn of glory elsewhere (Isa 60:1-2: 'arise, shine, for your light has come'). Here it merely marks the relentless repetition of the solar circuit. El-miqomo shoef zarach sham — 'hurrying to its place where it rises' — the verb sha'af means to pant or gasp (used for the panting of animals). The sun is personified as a gasping runner who cannot stop. The Teacher's rhetoric deliberately flattens what would normally be celebrated (sunrise) into evidence of endless futile repetition.
1.6: Holekh el-darom ve-sovev el-tzafon sovev sovev holekh haruach — 'The wind blows south, then turns north; it whirls around and around.' The word ruach (H7307) means both 'wind' and 'spirit/breath.' This double meaning runs through Ecclesiastes: the wind's aimless circulation (1:6) and 'chasing the wind' (1:14, re'ut ruach) anticipate the deep question of ch 3:21: 'Who knows whether the spirit [ruach] of man goes upward?' The same word that describes futile circular motion (wind circling) describes what is potentially immortal in the human person. Ve-al-sevivotav shav haruach — 'the wind returns to its circuits' — sevivotav from savav (to go around/turn), the same root as the wind's 'whirling'; there is no progress, only return.
1.7: Kol-hanechalim holkhim el-hayam ve-hayam einenu male — 'All the rivers run to the sea, yet the sea is never full.' The image is of an apparently purposeful process (rivers flowing toward an obvious destination) that produces no net result (the sea fills no further). El-maqom shehanechalim holkhim sham hem shavim laleshet — 'to the place where the rivers go, they return to flow again.' The verb shav (to return) describes both a physical cycle and a moral one throughout the wisdom literature: the righteous 'return' to God (Ps 90:3), the fool 'returns' to his folly (Prov 26:11). Here the rivers' return is merely hydrological — they evaporate, condense, and fall again — but the Teacher uses this mechanistic cycle as an image for the futility of all output that produces no permanent gain.
1.8: Kol-hadvarim ye'eim — 'All things are wearisome' or 'All words are wearisome.' The noun devarim (H1697) means both 'words/speech' and 'things/matters' — the ambiguity may be deliberate: both the things of life and the words used to describe them exhaust without satisfying. Lo yuchal ish ledaber — 'no one can fully express it' — the verb dibber connects back to devarim: the things are too wearisome to speak of, yet also the words are what prove insufficient. Lo-tiśba ayin lir'ot ve-lo-timale ozen mishmo'a — 'the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear full with hearing.' Sava (satisfied/full) links to the sea that is never 'full' (v.7, einenu male): the same Hebrew root family describes the fullness the sea never achieves and the satisfaction the eye never reaches.
1.9: Mah-shehayah hu sheyih-yeh — 'What has been is what will be.' The construction is emphatic repetition: shehayah (what has been) = sheyih-yeh (what will be). This is absolute cyclical determinism stated as wisdom observation. Ve-ein kol-chadash tahat hashemesh — 'there is nothing new under the sun.' Chadash (new) is the same root as in the covenant promise 'I will make a new covenant' (Jer 31:31) and the eschatological promise 'I make all things new' (Rev 21:5/Isa 43:19). The Teacher's claim is precisely bounded by his 'under the sun' frame — the new thing that breaks the cycle cannot appear within the closed system of the mortal world; the Teacher is right about the earthly frame and wrong only in that he cannot see beyond it.
1.10: Yesh davar sheyomer re'eh zeh chadash hu — 'Is there a thing of which one says, ‘See, this is new’?' The rhetorical question pattern is characteristic of Ecclesiastes' diatribe style: an imagined objector is entertained and then dismissed. Kebar hayah le'olamim — 'It has already been in the ages before us.' Olamim (plural of olam) = 'the ages' or 'antiquity' — time stretching back before memory. The Teacher's logic: what appears new to the present generation is merely beyond living memory (cf. v.11). This is observational wisdom, not philosophical nihilism; its limits become clear in v.11.
1.11: Ein zikaron larishonim — 'There is no remembrance of earlier people.' The noun zikaron (H2146) — memorial, remembrance, record — is related to zakhar (to remember), the root of the covenant promise 'I will remember my covenant' (Gen 9:15; Exod 2:24). YHWH's remembrance is what sustains the covenant when human memory fails. The Teacher identifies what is lost 'under the sun': no human mechanism exists to preserve memory across generations. Ve-gam barishonim shehyu — 'nor will there be among those yet to come' — the symmetry is complete: past generations are forgotten and future generations will forget us. This is the anthropological situation that the covenant's memorial practices (zikaron, the Passover, the Lord's Supper) exist to address.
1.12: Ani Qohelet hayiti melek al-Yisrael biYerushalayim — 'I, the Teacher, was king over Israel in Jerusalem.' The past tense hayiti ('I was') has puzzled interpreters: if the Teacher is currently king, why past tense? Some take it as a literary device (Qohelet is not actually Solomon, but a wisdom persona using his voice after-the-fact). Others read it as Solomon writing from old age looking back. The uncertainty is part of the book's rhetorical design: the Solomonic frame gives the experiment maximum epistemic authority (who could have done this experiment better?) without claiming to be Solomon's straightforward autobiography. The past tense emphasizes completed experience: 'I have been king — I know from the inside.'
1.13: Ve-natati et-libbi lidrosh ve-latour bahokmah — 'I applied my heart to seek and to search out by wisdom.' The verb pair darash (seek/inquire) + tur (explore/scout) describes intensive, systematic investigation — the same verbs used for the spies who scouted Canaan (Num 13:25). Inyan ra' hu asher natan Elohim livnei ha'adam la'anot bo — 'It is an unhappy business that God has given to the children of man to be busy with.' Inyan (H6045) is nearly unique to Ecclesiastes (occurs 8 times here, once in Sirach, nowhere else in the OT canon). It means business/occupation/task. The characterization of this investigating task as ra' (evil/unhappy) is the Teacher's verdict on what it is to be a questing human under the sun: God has given us a vocation of inquiry that, within the earthly frame, leads only to the recognition of limits.
1.14: Ra'iti et-kol-hama'asim sheassho tahat hashemesh — 'I have seen all the works that are done under the sun.' The verb ra'ah (to see/observe) is the Teacher's characteristic cognitive tool — used ~47 times in Ecclesiastes. He is an empiricist: he tests claims by observation. Re'ut ruach — 'chasing the wind' (or 'feeding on wind' — the verb ra'ah can mean 'to pasture/feed'). This phrase appears throughout chs. 1-6 as the verdict on each futile enterprise. The double meaning — chasing what cannot be caught, feeding on what has no substance — captures the Teacher's tone. The wind cannot be grabbed or accumulated; the labor that produces no lasting gain is as insubstantial as attempting to hold wind in one's fist.
1.15: Me'uvat lo-yuchal litkon — 'What is crooked cannot be made straight.' The verb taqan (to make straight/set right) appears only 3 times in the OT, two of them in Ecclesiastes (1:15; 7:13). The passive participle me'uvat (twisted/bent) describes a condition imposed from outside — the world as it currently is has been bent. The Teacher does not explain why the world is bent (that would require Gen 3's frame), but he recognizes the datum: within the 'under the sun' perspective, the crooked cannot be straightened by human wisdom. Ve-hemron lo yuchal lehimanol — 'and what is lacking cannot be counted' — hemron is a hapax legomenon (occurs once only); the sense is 'what is missing/deficient.' The Teacher identifies a fundamental insufficiency in the created order as he experiences it.
1.16: Hibarthi ani im-libbi lemor ani hineh higdalti ve-horafti hokmah al-kol asher-hayah lefanai al-Yerushalayim — 'I said to myself, “I have acquired more wisdom than anyone who ruled before me.”' The self-address (dibarthi ani im-libbi = 'I spoke with my heart') is one of Ecclesiastes' characteristic rhetorical devices; the 'heart' is the seat of the self, and the Teacher's internal dialogue becomes the structure of his investigation. The boast 'more than anyone before me in Jerusalem' maximizes the scope of the experiment: if Solomonic wisdom cannot find yitron, no wisdom can. Ve-libbi ra'ah harbeh hokmah ve-da'at — 'my heart has had great experience of wisdom and knowledge' — da'at (knowledge) is paired with hokmah (wisdom) as the two cognitive virtues; the Teacher has pursued both to the maximum.
1.17: Ve-etna libbi lada'at hokmah ve-lada'at holalot ve-sikhlet — 'I applied my heart to know wisdom, and to know madness and folly.' The pairing of wisdom with its opposites — holalot (madness/acting-foolishly) and sikhlet (folly/stupidity) — shows the Teacher's comprehensive methodology: he does not study wisdom in isolation but in contrast with its extremes. Yada'ti shegam-zeh hu ra'yon ruach — 'I perceived that this also is chasing the wind.' The word ra'yon is a variant form of re'ut (pursuit/striving) — both appear in Ecclesiastes for 'chasing the wind.' The conclusion: the pursuit of wisdom itself, as an end in itself, within the 'under the sun' frame, produces the same verdict. Wisdom is good but insufficient to achieve yitron.
1.18: Ki be-rov hokmah rov ka'as — 'For in much wisdom is much vexation.' The word ka'as (H3708) means grief, vexation, anger — the emotional state of frustration. Wisdom, the Teacher finds, does not deliver contentment; it delivers the expanded capacity to perceive what is wrong with the world, which is its own form of suffering. Ve-yosef da'at yosef makhov — 'and whoever increases knowledge increases sorrow.' The verb yosef (to add/increase) also spells the name Joseph — a word-play tradition observed in rabbinic commentary, though the primary meaning is straightforward. Makhov (H4341) means grief/pain — physical or emotional. The paradox: the cure (wisdom and knowledge) exacerbates the disease (awareness of futility). This is not anti-intellectualism but a precise observation about the limits of wisdom within the post-fall, sub-eschatological frame.

10.1: zevuvei mavet yavbish yabbia shemen roqeach: 'Dead flies cause the perfumer's ointment to give off a foul smell.' Zevuvei mavet (flies of death) — the construct chain places death inside the plural noun, making them death-flies rather than ordinary flies. Yabbish (cause to stink) and yabbia (cause to ferment/bubble over) — two causative verbs describing what a small contaminant does to the compound. Roqeach (perfumer/ointment-maker) — a specialized craftsman; the ointment represents accumulated skilled work that a trivial impurity destroys. Mikavod hochmah (more than honor [is] wisdom) — the second half uses a comparative min: a little folly outweighs wisdom-and-honor. The syntax forces the reader to hold the equivalence: much accumulated good, a little folly, and the folly wins.
10.2: lev chakham li-ymino ulev kesil li-smolo: 'The heart of the wise goes to the right, but the heart of the fool to the left.' Lev (heart) encompasses the whole inner orientation — not merely emotion but will, understanding, and character. Yamin (right) and smol (left) — in ANE symbolic geography, right = the place of honor, safety, favor (cf. Ps 110:1, God's right hand; Matt 25:33-34, sheep on the right). The proverb maps the interior orientation of the wise onto the culturally coded direction of honor and security, while the fool's heart navigates toward the side associated with dishonor and danger.
10.3: gam baderekh keshal libo holekh: 'Even while walking along the road, the fool's heart fails him.' Sekhel (wisdom, discernment, good sense) — the same root as maskil (one who understands). The fool walking on the road — not in any extraordinary situation but in the ordinary act of travel — demonstrates his lack of judgment (libo holekh, his heart goes; here indicating his character reveals itself in motion). Ve'amar lekhol sakhal hu: 'and he says of everyone that he is a fool' — the fool diagnoses others as foolish rather than examining himself. The projection of folly onto others is itself the demonstration of folly.
10.4: al tanach mimqomekha: 'Do not abandon your post.' Maqom (place, post, station) — the position of responsibility one holds under a superior. Marpe yanieach chatao'im gedolim: 'composure sets aside great offenses.' Marpe (composure, healing, calm) — the same root as marpe lashon (healing tongue) in Prov 12:18. Here it describes not verbal but behavioral composure under the ruler's anger. The wisdom insight: controlled non-reaction (marpe) has the power to defuse situations that reactive self-defense would escalate. The post is to be held because abandonment signals guilt and triggers exactly the pursuit the composed person avoids.
10.5: shegagah yotze'et milifnei hashalit: 'A blunder that proceeds from those in power.' Shegagah (unintentional mistake, inadvertent sin) — in the legal tradition, this is the term for accidental transgression requiring the atonement of Lev 4-5. Qohelet uses it for the category of governing blunder that is not malicious but produces serious harm anyway. Shallit — borrowed from Aramaic; used in Ecclesiastes for supreme authority. The verse observes that major social ills often originate not from deliberate wickedness but from the inadvertent failures of those with power — a shrewd political observation without parallel in the legal codes.
10.6: nittan hassekl bimromim rabbim: 'Fools are appointed to high positions.' Sekhel (folly, stupidity) as a nominalized form — the fool himself elevated to meromim rabbim (the heights, the great heights), while the ashirim (the capable, the wealthy) sit in lowly positions. The social inversion that Qohelet catalogues as ra'ah ra'iti (an evil I have seen) is the misallocation of authority — the wisdom tradition consistently argues that authority should track with character and competence; when it does not, the mismatch is a sign of social corruption rather than a stable new order.
10.7: ra'iti avadim al susim: 'I have seen servants riding on horseback.' Horses in the ancient world were the conveyance of military commanders and kings — not a transportation option for servants. The social reversal: servants (avadim) on horseback, princes (sarim) walking on foot. Kekhavadim (like servants) — the closing comparative locks the inversion: the princes walk in the manner appropriate to servants, having ceded the status-marker of mounted travel to those below them. Qohelet is documenting the breakdown of the social order whose disruption he traced to the governing blunder of verse 5.
10.8: kore shachat bah yipol: 'Whoever digs a pit may fall into it.' The pit-trap proverb appears in Prov 26:27 and Ps 7:15-16 — the trap intended for others becomes the trap of the trapper. Poretz gader yishkenu nachash: 'whoever breaks through a wall will be bitten by a snake.' Poretz gader (break through a hedge/wall) — the violation of a boundary-wall, whether property or covenant. Nachash (serpent/snake) — the snake in the wall is the consequence of boundary-breaking. Both proverbs in this verse are concrete observations about the recoil of aggression: the dug pit and the broken wall both return damage to their maker.
10.9: massa avenim ye'atsev bam: 'Whoever quarries stones may be hurt by them.' The labor-danger proverbs of vv.8-9 do not condemn the work but observe the inherent risk in every occupation. Baqea etzim yissaken bam: 'whoever splits wood is in danger from it.' Yissaken (is endangered/exposed to danger) — from the root connected to being set at risk or exposed. The four danger-proverbs (pit-digging, wall-breaking, stone-quarrying, wood-splitting) are Qohelet's way of observing that every form of activity aimed at altering or extracting from the natural order carries the possibility of reversal — the thing worked upon may work back.
10.10: im qehe habarzel velo pannim qilqal: 'If an axe is dull and you do not sharpen it.' Qilqal (to sharpen, lit. to make light/smooth) — the polishing of the blade's edge. Yegaber chayyalim: 'you will need to use much more strength.' Chayyalim (strengths, forces) — the intensification of labor required by a blunt instrument. Yitron hakshir chokhmah: 'wisdom is the advantage of skill.' Yitron (advantage, profit) — the key Ecclesiastes term for what endures and what gains. Here wisdom is identified with the pre-work sharpening that reduces the overall effort required: skill preparation is the yitron that multiplies effectiveness.
10.11: im yishokh hannachash belo lachash: 'If a snake bites before being charmed.' Lachash (charm, whisper — the whispering incantation of the snake charmer) — the professional practice of charming serpents by sound. Ve'ein yitron lebaal halashon: 'there is no profit for the master of the tongue.' Baal halashon (master of the tongue, lord of the tongue) — the one whose professional skill is verbal, including the snake charmer and, by extension, the advisor or orator who acts too late. If the snake has already bitten, no amount of verbal skill undoes it. Qohelet uses the wordplay: lachash (charm) and lashon (tongue) share consonants — the charmer's tongue that failed to act in time has no yitron.
10.12: divrei pi chakham chen: 'The words of the wise man's mouth are grace/goodwill.' Chen (grace, charm, favor, goodwill) — the social capital that wise speech creates. Uve-siftot kesil tevallenu: 'but the lips of a fool devour him.' Tevallenu (will swallow him, devour him) — the root bala used for swallowing. The wise man's speech creates grace for him; the fool's speech swallows him. The same instrument (the mouth/lips) produces opposite results depending on the character behind it — a concise statement of the tongue-wisdom that runs through the entire wisdom tradition.
10.13: techillat divrei pihu siklut: 'The beginning of his mouth's words is folly.' Siklut (folly, stupidity) — a characteristic Ecclesiastes word for the fullest form of human foolishness. Ve'acharit pihu ra'ah holelut: 'and the end of his mouth is wicked madness.' Holelut (madness, raving) — from halal (to praise/boast), here with negative valence: the raving self-praise of the unstable person. The progression is important: the fool begins in mere folly (cognitive error) and ends in ra'ah holelut (wicked madness) — the escalation of unchecked foolish speech. The end-state is not merely foolish but morally corrupted and psychologically unmoored.
10.14: vehassakal yarbeh devarim: 'The fool multiplies words.' Yarbeh devarim — the verbal excess of the fool is a consistent wisdom observation (Prov 10:19; Eccl 5:3). Lo yeda ha'adam mah sheyihyeh: 'a man does not know what will happen.' The epistemological limit is not the fool's alone but a universal condition — yet the fool speaks voluminously about the future as if he knows. Va'asher yihyeh me'acharayv mi yaggid lo: 'and who will tell him what will come after him?' The rhetorical question reinforces the point: the fool multiplies words about the unknowable. Qohelet consistently uses the lo yeda (does not know) formula to mark the limits that wisdom recognizes and folly ignores.
10.15: amal hakesilim teyaggenenu: 'The labor of fools wearies him.' The referent is singular (teyaggenenu, wearies him) though the subject is plural — Qohelet often uses the collective. The classic sign of extreme disorientation: asher lo yada lalekheth el-ir: 'one who does not know how to go to the city.' In ancient Palestine, cities were visible landmarks for navigation — not knowing how to reach the city is the paradigm of radical lostness. The fool exhausts himself in labor (amal) and still cannot manage basic orientation. The proverb is not about literal geography but about the fool's profound practical incompetence despite great effort.
10.16: oy lakh eretz shemalkeikh na'ar: 'Woe to you, land, whose king is a boy.' Na'ar (youth, boy) — not necessarily age but immaturity of character; the word covers both young age and immature judgment (cf. 1 Kgs 3:7 where Solomon calls himself na'ar at the start of his reign — not as a boast but as a confession of inadequacy). Vesaraiikh baboqer yokhelu: 'and your princes feast in the morning' — the morning feast signals that governing has been abandoned in favor of pleasure at the hour when officials should be conducting affairs of state.
10.17: ashreikh eretz shemalkeikh ben-chorim: 'Blessed is the land whose king is a son of freemen.' Ben-chorim (son of free people, son of nobles) — chorim derives from the root suggesting whiteness or nobility; but Qohelet uses it for character-nobility rather than bloodline. The contrast with na'ar (verse 16) is between immaturity-regardless-of-lineage and mature-character-regardless-of-lineage. Bishvul gevurah: 'for strength/self-mastery' — the princes eat at the proper time for strength, not for drunkenness. The same word gevurah (might) that describes divine power is here the quality of the self-disciplined official who eats to serve rather than to indulge.
10.18: beatslayim yimakh hamiqreh: 'Through great laziness the rafters sag.' Atslayim (double laziness, great sloth) — the intensified plural. Miqreh (rafter, roof-beam) — the structural support element. Ushiflut yadayim yidlof habayit: 'and through idle hands the house leaks.' The house neglected by idle hands leaks through its roof. Qohelet applies this as a political metaphor for the governing failures of vv.16-17: the land whose rulers are immature or self-indulgent is the house whose rafters sag — the structural decay of lazy governance is as predictable and visible as a leaking roof.
10.19: lishtok laasim lechem: 'A feast is prepared for laughter.' Lishtok (for laughter, for merriment) — the purpose of the feast. Vehayayin yesammach chayyim: 'and wine gladdens life.' Vehaakaseph ya'aneh et hakol: 'but money answers everything.' The verse is possibly ironic — the rulers of verse 16 prepare feasts for pleasure and claim wine gladdens life, but money provides for everything. The final clause (hakaseph ya'aneh et hakol) may be their rationalization: since money solves problems, the extravagant feast is justified. Qohelet's dry observation neither endorses nor fully condemns — he notes the rulers' own self-justifying logic as another expression of the governing malfunction he has been describing.
10.20: gam bemadakha al teqallel melek: 'Do not curse the king even in your intimate knowledge.' Madakha (your intimate thoughts, your knowledge/awareness) — the inner chamber of thought. Uvechadrei mishkavkha al teqallel ashir: 'even in the chambers of your bed do not curse the rich.' The rationale: ki of hashamayim yolich et haqol: 'for a bird of the heavens will carry the voice.' Tzipporah (bird) — the saying about bird-carried secrets may be a proverbial expression for information that spreads through informal channels. The practical wisdom: even private speech is not private. There is an economy of words even in thought and bedroom — the counsel to prudent speech extends to its most private forms.

11.1: shalach lachmekha al penei hamayim: 'Cast your bread upon the face of the waters.' Lachem (bread, food) — in the context of the wisdom tradition, the image may also evoke grain sent out in commerce (grain-ships on the Nile or Mediterranean). Al penei hamayim (upon the face of the waters) — the investment cast into the uncertain medium of trade or generosity, where outcomes are not controlled. Ki berov hayamim timtzaenu: 'for after many days you will find it again.' Berov hayamim (in the abundance of days, after many days) — the return is real but temporally deferred. The wisdom is about the willingness to release what one has into uncertain channels, trusting the return that cannot be predicted or controlled.
11.2: ten cheleq leshivah vegam lishmonah: 'Give a portion to seven, even to eight.' The surplus-giving beyond calculation: seven is a round number suggesting fullness; vegam lishmonah (and even to eight) pushes past the complete number, indicating generosity that exceeds what would be strictly required. Ki lo teda mah yihyeh ra'ah al ha'aretz: 'for you do not know what calamity may come upon the land.' The rationale for surplus generosity is epistemic — future calamity is unknown, and having distributed generously creates the relational and social network that sustains people through calamity. Generosity as an investment strategy under uncertainty.
11.3: im yimmal'u he'avim geshem al ha'aretz yariqu: 'If the clouds are full of rain, they pour it on the earth.' The cloud-rain example is of determined outcomes: filled clouds pour — there is no uncertainty. Ve'im yipol etz badrom o batzafon: 'and if a tree falls to the south or to the north.' Bimqom sheyyipol haetz sham yihyu: 'in the place where the tree falls, there it lies.' The fallen tree lies where it fell — past events are fixed. Together vv.3-4 frame the dilemma of action under uncertainty: some things are determined (clouds pour, trees stay where they fall), and the person who waits for perfect conditions (no wind, no clouds) will never sow or reap. Past fixity and future uncertainty together call for present action.
11.4: shomer ruach lo yizra: 'Whoever watches the wind will not sow.' Ruach (wind, spirit) — the dual meaning will be activated in verse 5. Here it is wind: the farmer who watches wind conditions looking for the perfect windless day will never sow his seed. Vero'eh be'avim lo yiqtzor: 'and whoever watches the clouds will not reap.' The weather-watcher who waits for certainty before acting produces nothing. The proverb commends action in the face of imperfect conditions rather than the paralysis of perpetual uncertainty-management. The person who never acts until all risks are eliminated makes the worst decision by default.
11.5: ka'asher einekha yode'a mah derech haruach: 'Just as you do not know the path of the wind.' Ruach here carries both meanings simultaneously — wind (the meteorological phenomenon) and spirit/breath (the animating principle). Ke'atzamot bebeten hamele'ah: 'like the bones in the womb of a pregnant woman' — the formation of the skeletal structure in utero. Kakah lo teda et ma'aseh ha'elohim asher ya'aseh et hakol: 'so you do not know the work of God who makes everything.' The two unknowable things — the path of the wind-spirit and the formation of bones in the womb — are both examples of God's creational work that proceeds beyond human observation. Qohelet uses this observation to motivate action despite ignorance rather than paralysis.
11.6: bazzoqer zera et zar'ekha: 'In the morning sow your seed.' Zera... zar'ekha — the verb and noun from the same root for maximal emphasis on the act of sowing. Velaerev al tanach yadekha: 'and in the evening do not let your hand rest.' The double-shift work ethic: sow morning and evening without ceasing. Ki einekha yode'a ei zeh yikshur: 'for you do not know which will succeed.' The epistemic uncertainty (lo yeda) that would paralyze the weather-watcher instead motivates the double-sower — precisely because you don't know which planting will prosper, you maximize both and trust one or both to produce. The o shneihem ke'echad tovim (or whether both will be equally good) is the hoped-for outcome of comprehensive diligence.
11.7: umetuq ha'or: 'Light is sweet.' Metuq (sweet) — sensory pleasure applied to sight. Vetov la'einayim lir'ot et hashemesh: 'and good for the eyes to see the sun.' This two-word observation (umetuq ha'or) opens the joy-section of 11:7-12:1 — the positive commendation of present delight before the aging and death section of chapter 12. The sweetness of light is not an argument for hedonism but a genuine acknowledgment of the goodness of created existence. Qohelet's recurring commendation of joy (simcha) is grounded in the goodness of the created world even within the context of its transience.
11.8: ki im shanah harbe yichyeh ha'adam: 'If a person lives many years.' Bekhullam yismach: 'in all of them let him rejoice.' The comprehensive-joy instruction: joy through the full length of life, not reserved for certain seasons. Veyizkor et yemei hachoshekh ki harbeh yihyu: 'but let him remember the days of darkness, for they will be many.' Yemei hachoshekh (days of darkness) — approaching death, the long undifferentiated darkness before the chapter 12 imagery. Kol sheba havel: 'all that comes is vapor.' The joy-and-remember structure: joy is commended, but the memory of darkness prevents the joy from becoming blind cheerfulness. Both are real.
11.9: simach bachur beyaldutekha: 'Rejoice, young man, in your youth.' Beyaldutekha (in your childhood/youth) — the unrecoverable time of youthful energy. Vetov libekha bimei bechurotekha: 'let your heart be good in the days of your prime.' Bechurot (prime of youth, vigor of youth) — the fullness of young strength. Vehalekh bidarkhei libekha uvemara'ei einekha: 'and walk in the ways of your heart and the sights of your eyes.' Qohelet endorses the full use of the young person's capacities — and then adds the crucial frame: veda ki al kol eleh yeviaakha ha'elohim bamishpat: 'and know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment.' The joy is real and commended; the accounting is also real and unavoidable.
11.10: vehasir ka'as milibekha: 'Banish anxiety from your heart.' Ka'as (vexation, irritation, anxiety — the gnawing frustration Qohelet has observed throughout) — here commanded away. Veha'aver ra'ah mibbesarekha: 'and put away pain from your body.' Ki heyaldut vehashachachar havel: 'for childhood and the prime of youth are vapor.' Shachachar (dawn, the first light) — a rare form; the youth-dawn connection suggests the fleeting first light of the day as the image for youth: beautiful, brief, already passing as it arrives. The verse closes the youth-section with the hevel verdict on youth itself — the vapor-breath that Qohelet has used throughout finally lands on the most energetic stage of human life.

12.1: uzekhor et borekha bimei bechurotekha: 'Remember your Creator in the days of your youth.' Borekha (your Creator) — plural of majesty from bara (to create). The command to zekor (remember) is the covenant call that runs through Deuteronomy — remembering is not merely cognitive recall but the active orientation of life toward what one knows. The bimei bechurotekha (in the days of your prime/youth) connects directly to 11:9's commendation of youth, adding the frame: youth is precisely the season for this orientation, before the deterioration described in the extended allegory that follows. Ad asher lo yavo'u yemei hara'ah: 'before the days of trouble come' — the aging and dying that chapter 12 elaborates.
12.2: ad asher lo techeshah hashemesh veha'or: 'Before the sun and the light grow dark.' The extended allegory of aging begins (vv.2-7), using cosmic and architectural imagery for the body's decline. The darkening of sun, light, moon, and stars has been interpreted as either a description of the old person's failing senses or as cosmic dissolution imagery. Veshabu he'avim achar haggeshem: 'and the clouds return after the rain' — unlike in summer when rain brings clearing, in old age the clouds return repeatedly, suggesting the relentless succession of ailments in old age where one illness follows another without full recovery in between.
12.3: beyom sheyir'azu shomrei habayit: 'In the day when the keepers of the house tremble.' The allegorical interpretation has a long tradition: shomrei habayit (keepers of the house) = arms/hands that tremble with age. Ve'hit'avtu anshei hachayil: 'and the men of valor become bent' = the legs that once bore the body's strength now bow. Uvatellu hatochnot ki me'etu: 'and the grinders cease because they are few' = the teeth. Vechashechu haro'ot ba'arubbot: 'and those who look through the windows grow dim' = the eyes. The body-house allegory transforms the physical decline of aging into a scene of architectural decay — simultaneously more dignified and more poignant than clinical description.
12.4: vesuggeru delatayim bashuk: 'When the street doors are shut' — possibly the mouth closed from lack of teeth (continuing the body-allegory), or the social withdrawal of old age. Bishfal qol hatachanah: 'when the sound of grinding is low' — the grinding mills (the teeth) silent. Veyaqum leqol hatzippur: 'when one rises at the sound of a bird' — the light sleep of the elderly who wake at any sound. Veyishshachu kol benot hashir: 'and all the daughters of song are brought low' — the voice and hearing fail; the singing and hearing of music that characterized the earlier seasons of life (11:9: the feast and joy) are diminished.
12.5: gam migavoha yira'u: 'And they will be afraid of heights.' Vechatchtim baderekh: 'and dread is on the path' — the confined world of the aged person who fears heights and dread every journey. Veyafetz hashaqed: 'when the almond tree blossoms' — the white blossoms on the bare branches of the almond tree in late winter, the first tree to blossom, are the image for white hair on an old head. Veyistabbal hechagav: 'when the locust drags itself along' — the labored movement of the aged. Vetafer ha'aviyyonah: 'and the caperberry fails' — possibly an aphrodisiac plant whose failure signals the end of sexual vitality. Ki holekh ha'adam el beit olamo: 'for man goes to his eternal home' — beit olamo (house of his eternity/the grave) — the destination toward which all the failing capacities converge.
12.6: ad asher lo yeratteq chevvel hakkesef: 'Before the silver cord is severed.' Chevvel hakesef (cord of silver) — the silver cord that breaks; in the body-allegory, possibly the spinal cord or the breath/life-thread. Vetaruts gullat hazahav: 'and the golden bowl is shattered' — the bowl of gold that cracks; the brain, or the lamp that illuminates. Veyishaver kad al hamayyin: 'and the pitcher is broken at the spring' — the lungs or heart at the spring of breath. Venarots hagalal el habor: 'and the wheel breaks at the cistern' — the pulley at the water-well, the heart or the circulatory system. The four broken-things images form a unified picture of the body's cessation — the cord, the bowl, the pitcher, the wheel — each representing a vital system that finally fails.
12.7: veyashov ha'afar al ha'aretz kashehayyah: 'The dust returns to the earth as it was.' The direct citation of Gen 3:19: ki afar atah ve'el afar tashuv (for you are dust and to dust you shall return). Qohelet's entire preceding allegory has been elaborating this single verse from Genesis. Veharuach tashuv el ha'elohim asher netanah: 'and the spirit returns to God who gave it.' The body returns to its material origin; the spirit (ruach) returns to its divine source. This is not a detailed theology of the afterlife but an acknowledgment that the life-breath is God's gift from beginning to end, returned to the Giver at death. The netanah (who gave it) frames the entire life between gift and return.
12.8: havel havalim amar haqohelet hakol havel: 'Vapor of vapors, says the Teacher — all is vapor.' The book closes (or near-closes) with the opening refrain of 1:2 — the inclusio that frames the entire work. Between the opening and closing havel havalim, Qohelet has observed kings and fools, wisdom and madness, labor and rest, feasts and funerals, youth and old age, the living and the dead. The final havel is not nihilistic despair but the considered conclusion of comprehensive observation: the transience of all things is the consistent finding of careful attention to reality.
12.9: veyoter shehayyah qohelet chakham: 'Beyond this, the Teacher was wise.' The epilogue (vv.9-14) shifts from first-person discourse to a third-person description of Qohelet's work. Od limmad da'at et ha'am: 'he also taught knowledge to the people.' Ve'izen vichiqer tiqen meshalim harbeh: 'he listened, investigated, and arranged many proverbs.' Izen (listened, weighed carefully) — from ozen (ear); the process of careful reception. Chiqer (searched, investigated) — from chaqar, to probe. Tiqen (arranged, set in order) — the final editorial work of giving structure to what was received and investigated. The three-stage wisdom process: receptive listening, active investigation, careful arrangement.
12.10: biqqesh qohelet limtzo divrei cheifetz: 'The Teacher sought to find pleasing words.' Divrei cheifetz (words of delight/pleasure) — not superficially entertaining but deeply satisfying words. Vekatuv yosher divrei emet: 'and written uprightly — words of truth.' Yosher (uprightness, honesty) and emet (truth, reliability) — the two standards that governed the Teacher's writing. The double commitment — to pleasing form and honest content — is the ideal of wisdom literature: the words should be both artistically satisfying and truthfully accurate. The form serves the content; the content requires honest investigation; both together are the cheifetz (delight) the Teacher sought.
12.11: divrei chakhamim kadarbenot: 'The words of the wise are like goads.' Darbenot (goads) — the pointed stick that moves animals; wisdom-words that prod the mind toward action. Uchemasmerot netu'im ba'alei asufot: 'and like firmly driven nails are the collected sayings.' Ba'alei asufot (masters of collections, members of collections) — the assembled body of wisdom sayings. Nittenu mere'eh echad: 'given by one Shepherd.' Ro'eh echad (one Shepherd) — the single source behind the multiple wisdom traditions. Qohelet here draws together all the wisdom teaching under the image of the one Shepherd — the divine source from whom all wisdom ultimately derives, regardless of its human compilers.
12.12: veyoter meheimah beni hizzaher: 'Beyond these, my son, be warned.' The beni (my son) address echoes the father-son frame of Proverbs 1-9 — wisdom tradition addressing the next generation. Asot sefarim harbeh ein qetz: 'making many books has no end.' The epistemological limit of the written corpus: books multiply without limit. Velagg limmud harbe yegi'at basar: 'and much study is weariness of the flesh.' The study that goes beyond the collected wisdom into endless book-making is identified as bodily exhaustion rather than the profitable labor (yitron amal) Qohelet has sought throughout. The warning is not against learning but against the mistaking of book-proliferation for wisdom.
12.13: sof davar hakol nishma: 'The conclusion of the matter — all has been heard.' Sof davar (the end of the matter, the conclusion) — the logical conclusion drawn from everything Qohelet has observed and said. Et ha'elohim yera ve'et mitzvotav shemor ki zeh kol ha'adam: 'Fear God and keep his commandments — for this is the whole of man.' Kol ha'adam (the whole of man, the entirety of man) — not merely the duty but the definition: this is what the human being finally is, in sum. The yirat elohim (fear of God) that Proverbs 1:7 places at the beginning as the start of wisdom, Qohelet places at the end as its conclusion — the beginning and end of the wisdom tradition converge on the same foundation.
12.14: ki et kol ma'aseh yavi ha'elohim bamishpat: 'For God will bring every deed into judgment.' Al kol ne'elam: 'concerning every hidden thing' — ne'elam (hidden, concealed) from alam (to be hidden/silent). The judgment is comprehensive: every action, im tov ve'im ra (whether good or whether evil). Qohelet's conclusion does not rest on epistemological humility alone (we don't know what will happen) but on the theological certainty that God's judgment will ultimately adjudicate what human observation cannot reach. The hidden things that escape human reckoning do not escape the divine accounting. This final verse transforms the entire book's observations about the limits of human knowledge into an argument for moral seriousness under divine sovereignty.

2.1: Amarthi ani be-libbi lekha na' anasekha be-simha ure'eh be-tov — 'Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy yourself.' The verb nasah (to test/try) introduces the pleasure experiment: the Teacher applies his empirical method to hedonism. Be-simha (in pleasure/joy) — this word simha (H8057) later becomes the Teacher's positive counsel (2:10, 26; 3:12, 22; etc.): joy in one's work is God's gift. Here the Teacher tests whether pleasure as a comprehensive life-strategy produces yitron — it does not. Ve-hineh gam-hu havel — 'but this also was futility.' The structural formula 'this also was hevel' (or 'this also is chasing the wind') marks each section of the Royal Experiment with the same verdict.
2.2: Le-sikhok amarthi mehulal — 'I said of laughter, “It is madness.”' Mehulal is the pual (passive intensive) participle of halal (to praise/to be foolish) — meaning 'madness' in this negative sense. The same root gives the common word hallelujah (praise the LORD): laughter here is the degenerate form of what was made for praise. Ve-la-simha mah-zoh oset — 'and of pleasure, “What does this achieve?”' The question uses oset from asah (to do/make) — what does pleasure make? The implication: pleasure is sterile, it produces nothing.
2.3: Tarthi be-libbi limshokh bayain et-besari — 'I searched with my heart how to cheer myself with wine.' Tarthi is from tur (to explore/scout) — the same verb as 1:13; this is systematic exploration, not mere indulgence. Ve-libbi noheig ba-hokmah — 'my heart guiding me with wisdom' — the Teacher's method is crucial: he does not abandon wisdom while exploring pleasure but uses wisdom as the guide. This is the meaning of 'testing' (nasah) pleasure: a wisdom-governed experiment, not mere hedonism. Ve-le'ehaz be-sikhlet — 'and how to take hold of folly' — taking hold of folly while maintaining wisdom is the experimental design. Ad asher ere mah-tov livnei ha'adam asher ya'asu tahat hashamayim — 'until I might see what was good for the children of man to do under heaven during the few days of their life.' Note tahat hashamayim (under heaven) rather than tahat hashemesh (under the sun) — a slight variation; 'heaven' emphasizes the dome above while 'sun' emphasizes the light source.
2.4: Higdalti ma'asai baniti li batim — 'I made great works: I built houses for myself.' The self-referential li ('for myself') appears repeatedly in vv. 4-8 — a deliberate accumulation: the Royal Experiment is characterized by self-oriented acquisition. Nata'ti li keramim — 'I planted vineyards for myself.' The scale of the experiment is Solomonic: 1 Kings 7 describes Solomon's building projects; the Teacher claims to have done all of it. The self-reference also sets up the verdict: 'for myself' is precisely the orientation that the Teacher discovers is futile — it produces yitron for no one when death transfers it all.
2.5: Asiti li ganot u-pardesim — 'I made gardens and parks for myself.' The word pardes (H6508) is a loanword from Persian (pairi-daeza = enclosed garden/park) — one of the OT's few Persian loanwords, suggesting the book reached its final form in the Persian period. In the NT, paradeisos (garden of God, Paradise) derives from this same Persian root via Greek (Luke 23:43; Rev 2:7). The Teacher's constructed pleasure garden is a shadow of what was lost in Eden and promised in the new creation. Ve-nata'ti bahem etz kol-peri — 'and planted in them all kinds of fruit trees' — the abundance of Eden (Gen 2:9: 'every tree') echoes in the Teacher's constructed garden; the human impulse to recreate paradise is precisely what the experiment tests.
2.6: Asiti li berekot mayim — 'I made pools from which to water the forest of growing trees.' The act of water management — pools fed from springs to irrigate an artificial forest (ya'ar) — requires comprehensive control over the environment: engineering, labor, resource. The scale of hydraulic infrastructure in the Ancient Near East was a sign of royal power (cf. Hezekiah's tunnel, 2 Kgs 20:20). The Teacher lists this achievement as evidence of his experimental scope, then negates it entirely in v.11.
2.7: Qaniti avadim ve-shfachot — 'I bought male and female servants.' The verb qanah (to buy/acquire) is the same word disputed at Prov 8:22 ('the LORD possessed/created me'). Here it unambiguously means commercial acquisition. The Teacher's accumulation of servants, livestock, and wealth is the comprehensive test of whether material advantage creates lasting gain. Gam-miqneh baqar va-tzon harbeh hayah li mi-kol shehayu lefanai biYerushalayim — 'I also had great herds of cattle and flocks of sheep — more than anyone before me in Jerusalem.' The superlative ('more than anyone before me') is the marker of an experiment that covers the maximum possible scope; any lesser accumulation could be dismissed as not truly testing the question.
2.8: Kafatsi li gam-kesef ve-zahav — 'I also gathered for myself silver and gold.' Kafatsi is from kafah — to skim/scrape together; the word emphasizes accumulation by relentless gathering. Segulat melakhim ve-hamdinot — 'the treasures of kings and provinces.' Segulah (H5459) is the covenant word for Israel as God's 'treasured possession' (Exod 19:5; Deut 7:6; Mal 3:17). Here it refers to material wealth — the Teacher has accumulated the nations' treasures as an experiment. The word's covenant resonance (Israel as God's segulah) creates ironic contrast: the Teacher collects a segulah of material goods, which will prove hevel; the divine segulah of a redeemed people is what cannot be declared vanity.
2.9: Ve-gadalthi ve-hosafti mi-kol asher-hayah lefanai biYerushalayim — 'So I became greater than all who were before me in Jerusalem.' Gadal (to become great) and yasaf (to add/increase) describe compound growth. Af-hokmati amdah li — 'and my wisdom also remained with me.' This is the key methodological claim: the Teacher did not abandon wisdom during the pleasure experiment. He did not become a hedonist; he maintained his capacity for evaluation. The experiment's design requires this: only someone who retains wisdom can accurately evaluate whether pleasure produces yitron.
2.10: Ve-khol asher sha'alu einai lo atzalthi mehem — 'And whatever my eyes desired I did not withhold from them.' The verb atzal (to withhold) is the same root used of Abraham who 'did not withhold' his son Isaac (Gen 22:12, lo chatsakta). The Teacher's self-indulgence is total: nothing the eye desired was refused. Ki-libbi samach mi-kol amali ve-zeh hayah helqi mi-kol amali — 'for my heart found pleasure in all my toil — and this was my reward for all my toil.' Helqi (H2506) — my portion/share — is a key word in Ecclesiastes' positive counsel. The Teacher discovers that joy in the process of work is itself the reward: not the product accumulated, but the experience of working with pleasure is the helqi, the portion that actually belongs to one.
2.11: Ve-faniti ani be-khol-ma'asai sheasu yadai — 'Then I considered all that my hands had done.' The verb panah (to turn/face) describes a deliberate reorientation of attention: the Teacher turns to evaluate what the accumulation experiment has produced. Ve-hineh hakol havel ure'ut ruach — 'all was futility and chasing the wind.' Re'ut ruach — the MKT renders this 'chasing the wind'; the word re'ut (from ra'ah, to feed/shepherd) might mean 'feeding on wind' — consuming what has no substance. Ve-ein yitron tahat hashemesh — 'there was nothing to be gained under the sun.' The exact phrase from 1:3 (mah yitron) closes this subsection: the experiment yields the predicted result. The repetition is structural, not literary laziness.
2.12: Ve-faniti ani lir'ot hokmah ve-holalot ve-sikhlet — 'So I turned to consider wisdom, madness, and folly.' The Teacher pivots from the Royal Experiment to comparative reflection. Ki mah ha'adam sheyavo ahar hamelekh — 'For what can the man who comes after the king do?' The question has been read as: (a) no one can replicate the royal experiment at this scale, or (b) the successor king (3:16-4:16 takes up this motif) faces the same vanity. Et asher-kvar ashuhu — 'only what has already been done' — the heir inherits the fruits without the process, and can add nothing beyond what the royal accumulator has already done. This connects to the 'no new thing' (1:9) motif.
2.13: Ve-ra'iti ani she-yesh yitron la-hokmah min-ha-sikhlet — 'Then I saw that wisdom excels folly.' The word yitron (advantage/profit) here describes wisdom's superiority over folly — but notice the context: wisdom has yitron, but this yitron is not the same as the ultimate yitron under the sun that the book is seeking. Wisdom is genuinely better than folly; the Teacher does not deny this. Ke-yitron ha'or min-ha-choshekh — 'as light excels darkness.' The comparison (ke-yitron = 'as the advantage of') is an analogy: wisdom over folly is like light over darkness. But as vv. 14-16 will show, this advantage does not survive the one event that equalizes both wise and foolish: death.
2.14: He-chakham einav be-rosho ve-ha-kesil ba-choshekh holekh — 'The wise person has his eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness.' The idiom 'eyes in his head' means attentiveness and perceptive awareness — the wise person sees where he is going. The fool (kesil, H3684 = the dull/stubborn fool, one who acts against known wisdom) walks in the dark. Ve-yadati gam-ani she-miqreh echad yiqreh et-kulam — 'Yet I also perceived that the same event happens to all of them.' The word miqreh (H4745) — 'event/fate/lot' — is key to Ecclesiastes' anthropology. It occurs 7 times (2:14, 15; 3:19 [x3]; 9:2, 3) and always describes the common death that erases all distinctions. Wisdom's advantage (eyes in the head) does not alter miqreh.
2.15: Ve-amarthi ani be-libbi ke-miqreh ha-kesil gam-ani yiqreh li — 'What happens to the fool will happen to me also.' The Teacher's logic is unsentimental: if both the wise and the foolish die the same death, what is the practical yitron of wisdom in the long run? La-mah chakhamti ani az yoter — 'So why was I so very wise?' Yoter (H3148) means 'exceedingly/more/exceeding' — the superlative wisdom experiment was pointless if death erases the advantage. Ve-dibarti be-libbi she-gam-zeh havel — 'and I said in my heart that this also is futility.' The self-address formula again; the Teacher's internal monologue is the structure of the experiment's evaluation.
2.16: Ki ein zikaron le-chakham im-ha-kesil le'olam — 'For there is no lasting remembrance of the wise any more than of the fool.' The word zikaron (memorial/remembrance) carries the weight of the covenant memorial system: what YHWH 'remembers' is sustained; what has no zikaron passes from existence as if it had never been. The Teacher's point: both wise and foolish leave no permanent human memorial; future generations will not distinguish them. Ve-eikh yamut he-chakham im-ha-kesil — 'How does the wise man die? Just like the fool.' The parallel death equalizes all human achievement. The Teacher is not denying that wisdom is better (v.13); he is denying that wisdom achieves the immortality of the memorial that would constitute yitron.
2.17: Ve-sane'ti et-ha-chayyim — 'So I hated life.' The verb sane' (to hate) is strong: the Teacher has moved from frustration to something like existential revulsion. This is the emotional nadir of the Royal Experiment — not a philosophical conclusion but a felt response to the futility of all he has accumulated and observed. Ki ra alay hama'aseh sheassoh tahat hashemesh — 'because what is done under the sun seemed evil to me.' Ra (evil/bad) is the Teacher's evaluative word for what the futility-saturated world presents. Ki hakol havel ure'ut ruach — 'for all is futility and chasing the wind.' The full verdict formula closes the subsection. The Teacher's 'hatred of life' is not suicidal — it is the honest recognition that life 'under the sun,' without eschatological frame, is insufficient ground for sustained joy.
2.18: Ve-sane'ti ani et-kol amali sheani amel tahat hashemesh — 'And I hated all my toil in which I had labored under the sun.' The word amel (H5998, to labor/toil) gives the noun amal (H5999) — used 35 times in Ecclesiastes; it means both the activity of laboring and the product of that labor. She-ani amel tahat hashemesh — 'in which I had labored under the sun' — the repetition of the laboring verb emphasizes the investment of self. She-eni'chennu la'adam sheyihyeh acharai — 'because I must leave it to the man who comes after me.' The problem of inheritance: the accumulator cannot control what the heir does with what is inherited. The wealth passes, but not the wisdom that produced it. This is one of the book's most humanly piercing observations about death and its consequences.
2.19: U-mi yode'a ha-yih-yeh chakham o sakal — 'And who knows whether he will be wise or foolish?' The phrase mi yode'a ('who knows?') is a characteristic Ecclesiastes interrogative marking epistemological uncertainty (cf. 3:21; 6:12; 8:1). The heir might be a wise steward of the inheritance or a fool who dissipates it — the accumulator cannot know. Vi-yishlet be-khol amali — 'yet he will have control over all the fruit of my toil' — the verb shalat (to have dominion/control) echoes Gen 1:28 (radah/dominion); the heir exercises dominion over what the accumulator's wisdom produced. Gam-zeh havel — 'this also is futility.' The brevity of the conclusion matches the bitterness of the insight: a single phrase marks one of the human condition's most frustrating realities.
2.20: Ve-savothi ani le-ya'esh libbi — 'So I turned about and gave my heart up to despair.' Savothi (from savav, to go around/turn) recalls the circular motions of 1:6 (the wind whirling), now applied to the Teacher himself: he turns, as everything turns, in a cycle that goes nowhere. Ya'esh (despair/hopelessness) — the noun is used only twice in the OT (here and Job 17:11). Al-kol ha-amal sheamalti tahat hashemesh — 'over all the toil at which I had labored under the sun.' The preposition al ('over') with despair: the Teacher's despair is directed specifically at the accumulated labor, not at life generically. His despair is as precise as his analysis.
2.21: Ki yesh adam she-amalo be-hokmah uve-da'at uve-kishron — 'Because sometimes a person who labored with wisdom and knowledge and skill.' The triple characterization — wisdom, knowledge, skill (kishron, H3788, efficiency/advantage) — describes the ideal worker. Ve-le'adam shelo amal-bo yitnennu helqo — 'must leave everything to someone who did not work for it at all.' Helqo (his portion) — the concept of helq reappears: the heir receives a helq he did not earn, while the accumulator's portion (helq) that he earned through wisdom goes with him to death. Gam-zeh havel vera'ah rabbah — 'this also is futility and great evil.' The formula intensifies: not just havel but vera'ah rabbah (great evil) — the unjust distribution of the fruits of wisdom is the worst category the Teacher has yet assigned.
2.22: Ki mah-hoveh la'adam be-khol amalo — 'For what does a man get from all his toil?' The word hoveh (H1933) — 'what comes to' or 'what befalls' — is an unusual form; some manuscripts read yih-yeh (what will be). The question is the same as 1:3 (mah yitron) but sharpened: not 'what lasting gain' but 'what actually arrives/comes to the man?' U-vera'yon libbi shehu amel tahat hashemesh — 'and striving of heart with which he labors under the sun.' Ra'yon libbi (striving of his heart) — the word ra'yon is related to re'ut ruach (chasing the wind): the inner striving and the outer metaphor are from the same root.
2.23: Ki khol-yamav makhavim ve-ka'as inyano — 'For all his days are full of sorrow, and his work is grief.' Makhavim (sorrows/pains) — plural of makhov (H4341, grief/pain), same word as 1:18: the Teacher returns to his earlier observation. Inyan again: the 'business' of life is grief. Gam-ba-layla lo-shakhav libboh — 'even at night his heart does not rest.' The heart (lev) is the organ of thought and evaluation in Hebrew psychology; it does not stop working even when the body rests. Gam-zeh havel hu — 'this also is futility.' The relentlessness of sorrow — no rest even at night — is itself characterized as hevel.
2.24: Ein tov ba'adam sheyokhal ve-shata ve-hera'ah et-nafsho be-amalo — 'There is nothing better for a person than to eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil.' The phrase ein tov ('there is nothing better') introduces the carpe diem counsel that recurs throughout Ecclesiastes (3:12, 22; 5:18; 8:15; 9:7-9). The MKT's 'find enjoyment in his toil' renders hera'ah et-nafsho — literally, 'to cause his soul/life to see good' — the nefesh (H5315, soul/life/self) finding its experience of the good. Gam-zoh ra'iti ani ki mi-yad ha'Elohim hi — 'This also, I saw, is from the hand of God.' The pivot: the carpe diem counsel is not mere hedonism but a recognition that the capacity for enjoyment is itself a divine gift (mi-yad ha'Elohim, 'from God's hand' — the hand that provides).
2.25: Ki mi yokhal ve-mi yachush chutz mimmenni — 'For apart from God, who can eat or find enjoyment?' The MT reads mimmenni ('apart from me') — a first-person form — but the LXX reads mimmennu ('apart from him,' i.e., God). Most English versions follow the LXX here. The MT reading (the Teacher speaking in his own voice) would mean: 'who can enjoy apart from what I have experienced?' — a claim that the experiment was comprehensive. Either reading supports the theological point: enjoyment is not self-generated; it requires a giver (v.24: 'from the hand of God') or a framework (the Teacher's wisdom). The verse is brief — a single rhetorical question — where the point is made entirely by the question's force.
2.26: Ki le-adam she-tov lefanav natan hokmah ve-da'at ve-simha — 'For God gives wisdom, knowledge, and joy to the one who pleases him.' The triple gift (wisdom, knowledge, joy) to the one who 'is good before him' (tov lefanav) — the idiom 'good before God' means morally acceptable, pleasing to God's sight. Ve-la-chote natan inyan le'esof ule-khnas — 'but to the sinner he assigns the task of gathering and accumulating.' The sinner's inyan (business) is le'esof ule-khnas (to gather and to accumulate) — both verbs describe hoarding. Litet le-tov lifnei ha'Elohim — 'only to hand it over to the one who pleases God.' The ironic reversal: the sinner labors to accumulate, then transfers it to the God-pleaser at death. Gam-zeh havel ure'ut ruach — the full verdict formula closes the chapter.

3.1: Le-khol ze-man ve-et le-khol-chefetz tahat hashamayim — 'For everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose under heaven.' The poem of ch. 3 opens with two near-synonyms: ze-man (H2165, an appointed time/fixed time) and et (H6256, a time/occasion). Ze-man is the more precise, carrying the sense of a set appointment (related to zamam, to plan/purpose). Chefetz (H2656) means 'purpose/delight/desire' — what one delights in or intends. The verse frames the poem: it is not randomness but a structured schedule of appointments that governs all activity. The question the poem poses is who sets that schedule.
3.2: Et laledet ve-et lamut — 'A time to be born, and a time to die.' The 14-pair poem (vv.2-8) uses a merism structure: naming the two extremes implies all that lies between. Birth and death are the ultimate brackets of human life. Et lata'at ve-et la'akor natu'a — 'a time to plant, and a time to uproot.' The agricultural verbs shift the focus: if birth/death are biological facts, planting/uprooting are volitional — human decisions about when to invest and when to abandon. Both pairs fall within the ze-man/et structure: all human decisions, not just biological events, have their appointed time.
3.3: Et laharog ve-et lirpo — 'A time to kill, and a time to heal.' The verb harag (to kill/slay) is used for both murder and legitimate killing; context determines the moral valence. The Teacher does not categorize: the poem is descriptive of the range of human activities, not prescriptive of which are justified. Et lifrotz ve-et livnot — 'a time to break down, and a time to build up.' The pair describes demolition and construction — both are valid activities within the appointed seasons. The poem does not say which is preferable in the abstract; each has its time.
3.4: Et livkot ve-et liskhok — 'A time to weep, and a time to laugh.' The emotional pairs: grief and joy, mourning and dancing. Jesus cites this kind of wisdom (implicitly) when he says 'Blessed are those who mourn' (Matt 5:4) and 'Blessed are you who weep now, for you shall laugh' (Luke 6:21). The Teacher observes the structure of appointed emotional seasons; Jesus announces the eschatological reversal that the Teacher, 'under the sun,' cannot see. Et safod ve-et rekhod — 'a time to mourn, and a time to dance.' Safad (to mourn/beat the breast) and raqad (to dance/leap) are the emotional extremes of communal response — mourning at funerals, dancing at festivals.
3.5: Et le-hashlikh avanim ve-et knos avanim — 'A time to throw away stones, and a time to gather stones.' This pair has generated the most interpretive debate in the poem — the practice of throwing and gathering stones is not self-evidently meaningful. Ancient interpretations include: (1) agricultural land clearing (throwing stones from a field) and gathering them for building; (2) a euphemism for sexual activity (rabbinic); (3) military sieges (throwing stones at fortified cities) and reconstruction after them. The ambiguity is likely intentional — it resists resolution, illustrating the poem's point about inscrutable timing. Et lavkot ve-et lirhod — 'a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing' — physical intimacy has its appointed time.
3.6: Et levaqesh ve-et le'abed — 'A time to seek, and a time to lose.' The pair contrasts active seeking (baqash, to seek/search) with passive loss (abad, to perish/be lost). The choice to seek and the fact of loss both fall within appointed seasons. Et lishmor ve-et le-hashlikh — 'a time to keep, and a time to throw away.' The pair of shamar (to keep/guard — the same verb as keeping covenant, Exod 20:6) and hashlikh (to throw/cast away) extends the stone-gathering/throwing pair: sometimes what one has faithfully kept must be surrendered.
3.7: Et li-qro'a ve-et litfor — 'A time to tear, and a time to sew.' Tearing garments was the ancient gesture of grief (Gen 37:29; Job 1:20); sewing them represented the end of mourning. The pair encapsulates the rhythm of grief and recovery. Et la-hachshot ve-et le-dabber — 'a time to keep silence, and a time to speak.' Wisdom literature consistently values the discipline of silence: 'He who restrains his words has knowledge' (Prov 17:27). But silence and speech each have their appointed time — the wisdom is in discerning which the moment calls for.
3.8: Et le'ehov ve-et lisno — 'A time to love, and a time to hate.' The climactic final pair of human relationships. Love and hate are presented without moral commentary: the poem describes the full range of human relational experience as having an appointed time structure. Et milchamah ve-et shalom — 'a time for war, and a time for peace.' The social/political level: war and peace are not merely human choices but fall within the structured seasons of the cosmos. The poem closes at the political macro-level after opening with the biological micro-level (birth and death), encompassing the full range of human experience.
3.9: Mah-yitron ha-oseh ba'asher hu amel — 'What gain does the worker have from his toil?' The question of 1:3 (mah yitron) recurs after the poem — but now the context is different. The poem has shown that all activities have their appointed time; the question returns: if all things have their time and everything cycles, what lasting advantage accumulates? The question is answered in vv.10-15: there is no yitron from the worker's perspective, but God's perspective gives meaning to what appears cyclical and futile.
3.10: Ra'iti et-ha-inyan asher natan Elohim livnei ha-adam la'anot bo — 'I have seen the business that God has given to the children of man to be occupied with.' The word inyan recurs (cf. 1:13): 'unhappy business.' The repetition links this reflection to the opening investigation: the same inyan that God assigned (1:13) is the subject of continued observation. The Teacher's empirical method — seeing, observing, examining — governs his procedure throughout.
3.11: Et-hakol asah yafeh be-ito — 'He has made everything beautiful in its time.' The word yafeh (beautiful/appropriate) is the same root as the superlative of the Song of Songs (1:15-16: 'you are beautiful'). God's making of 'everything beautiful in its time' affirms the goodness of the appointed schedule — each thing is beautiful precisely when it occurs in its proper time. Gam et-ha-olam natan be-libbam — 'Also, he has put eternity in the human heart.' Olam (H5769) — normally 'forever/eternity/age' — is placed 'in the human heart' as a divine implantation. This is the theological key to why the Teacher's investigation is frustrated: humans have an awareness of eternity (olam) but cannot 'find out what God has done from beginning to end.' The olam-capacity creates the longing; the mortality creates the frustration. The NT's resolution is resurrection: in Christ, the olam-longing is met eschatologically.
3.12: Yada'ti ki ein tov bam ki im-lismoch ve-la'asot tov be-chayyav — 'I know that there is nothing better for them than to rejoice and to do good throughout their life.' The carpe diem counsel now includes an ethical dimension: ve-la'asot tov ('and to do good'). Enjoyment and goodness are paired — the positive counsel is not hedonism but joyful moral living. The addition of 'do good' distinguishes Ecclesiastes' counsel from mere pleasure-seeking: the appointed seasons include seasons for ethical action, not just personal enjoyment. Be-chayyav — 'throughout his life' — the temporal scope: not a single moment of pleasure but a stance toward the whole of life.
3.13: Ve-gam kol-ha-adam sheyokhal ve-shata ve-ra'ah tov be-khol amalo — 'And that everyone should eat and drink and take pleasure in his toil.' The formula from 2:24 recurs with slight variation: 'every person' (kol-ha-adam) broadens the counsel from the singular experiment to a universal principle. Matat Elohim hi — 'this is God's gift.' Matat (H4991, gift) from natan (to give) — the capacity for enjoyment is a divine donation, not a human achievement. The passive construction 'is God's gift' is stronger than 2:24's 'from the hand of God': the gift character of enjoyment is now stated as a theological proposition, not just an observation.
3.14: Yada'ti ki kol-asher ya'aseh ha'Elohim hu yih-yeh le'olam — 'I know that whatever God does endures forever.' Le'olam (forever) — God's acts are characterized by the olam permanence that human acts lack. Ein le-hosif alav ve-ein ligro'a mimmenhu — 'nothing can be added to it and nothing taken away from it.' This is the ontological description of divine sovereignty over creation: God's acts are complete and unalterable. Ve-ha'Elohim asah she-yir'u milfanav — 'God has done it so that people would fear him.' The purpose of divine immutability is the fear of God (yir'ah, H3374) — the reverential awe that is the beginning of wisdom (Prov 1:7). God's unchangeability, rather than making him remote, creates the conditions for appropriate human response.
3.15: Mah-shehayah kebar hu — 'What is has already been.' The cyclical observation of 1:9 is restated but now in a theological frame: it is not merely a human observation about repetition but a characterization of how God relates to history. Ve-asher lih-yot kebar hayah ve-ha'Elohim yevaqesh et-nirdaf — 'and what is to be already has been. God seeks out what has been driven away.' The final phrase is the most difficult in this verse: et-nirdaf (H7291, the hunted/driven away/pursued one) — God 'seeks' what has been pushed aside, marginalized, or seemingly lost. Some read this as a recycling of the past; others as God pursuing justice for the driven-away (the oppressed, cf. 3:16). The theological claim is that God's sovereignty over time means nothing falls permanently outside his attention.
3.16: Ve-od ra'iti tahat hashemesh meqom ha-mishpat shamah ha-resha — 'Moreover, I saw under the sun that in the place of justice, even there was wickedness.' Mishpat (H4941, justice/judgment) and tzedeq (righteousness) are the two pillars of YHWH's throne (Ps 89:14); here both are perverted in the institutions (maqom, place) designed to embody them. The corruption of the court system — the place of justice becoming the place of wickedness — is the most socially devastating form of hevel: the system designed to protect the oppressed becomes a tool of oppression. The Teacher does not call for revolution; he observes and waits for v.17's resolution.
3.17: Amarthi ani be-libbi et-ha-tzadiq ve-et-ha-rasha yishpot ha'Elohim — 'I said in my heart, “God will judge the righteous and the wicked.”' The theological response to v.16's corruption is not despair but confidence in divine judgment. Ki et le-khol-chefetz ve-al kol-hama'aseh sham — 'for there is a time for every matter and for every work.' The word sham ('there') is puzzling: where is 'there'? Some read it as 'then' (temporal); others as a reference to the divine court. The appointed-time structure of the poem (3:1-8) returns: the corruption of human courts will have its appointed time of divine judgment. The et/ze-man structure that governs all things governs judgment too.
3.18: Amarthi ani be-libbi al-dibrat benei ha-adam — 'I said in my heart with regard to the children of man.' Dibrat (H1700) — rare word; 'on account of/concerning/for the sake of' — the Teacher's reflection is directed specifically at the human situation. Levar'am ha'Elohim ve-lir'ot she-hem-behemah hemah lahem — 'that God is testing them so they may see that they themselves are but animals.' The verb berar (H1305, to sift/test/purify) appears in a reflexive form: God is separating/testing humanity to show them something about themselves. The word behemah (beast/animal, H929) is the sharp term: the Teacher makes the unsentimental claim that from a sub-eschatological perspective, humans and beasts share the same creaturely mortality.
3.19: Ki miqreh benei ha-adam u-miqreh ha-behemah u-miqreh echad lahem — 'For what happens to the children of man is what happens to the beasts; the same thing happens to both.' The word miqreh (event/fate) appears three times in this verse — the repetition is emphatic. Ke-mot zeh ken mot zeh — 'As one dies, so dies the other.' The brevity of the construction heightens the impact: no expansion, no qualification. Ve-ruach echad le-khol — 'They all have the same breath/spirit.' Ruach echad (one spirit/breath) — the single ruach that gives life to both human and animal, and whose departure means death for both. U-motar ha-adam min-ha-behemah ayin — 'man has no advantage over the beasts' — motar (H4195, advantage/profit) is yet another word in the yitron family; from the perspective of mortality alone, there is no human advantage. Ki hakol havel — 'All is futility.' The only verse in this section that ends with havel alone, without 're'ut ruach.'
3.20: Hakol holekh el-maqom echad — 'All go to one place.' Maqom echad (one place) — the shared destination of all mortals: the grave, Sheol. The universality is total: not 'some' or 'most' but 'all.' Hakol hayah min-ha'afar ve-hakol shav el-ha'afar — 'All are from the dust, and all return to dust.' The echo of Genesis 3:19 is unmistakable (ki afar atah ve-el-afar tashuv: 'for you are dust, and to dust you shall return') — the Teacher is applying the curse of Gen 3 to the full range of created mortality. The Teacher does not cite Genesis, but his observation is rooted in the same creaturely condition that the fall confirmed.
3.21: Mi yode'a ruach benei ha-adam ha-olah hi lema'lah — 'Who knows whether the spirit of man goes upward?' The mi yode'a ('who knows?') formula marks the limit of 'under the sun' knowledge. The question about the destiny of the human ruach is left open: goes upward (to God) or downward (like the animal's spirit, to the earth)? Ve-ruach ha-behemah hayoredet hi lemattah la'aretz — 'and the spirit of the beast goes downward to the earth?' The Teacher does not deny the resurrection — he identifies it as beyond the epistemological reach of sub-eschatological wisdom. The NT's announcement of resurrection is the answer to precisely this mi yode'a: the resurrection of Christ demonstrates that the human ruach does indeed have an upward destiny, because Christ's has shown the way (1 Cor 15:20).
3.22: Ve-ra'iti ki ein tov me-asher yismach ha-adam be-ma'asav — 'So I saw that there is nothing better than that a man should rejoice in his work.' The carpe diem counsel recurs, now grounded in the preceding meditation on mortality and divine judgment. Rejoicing in work is the positive response to the Teacher's epistemological limit: since 'who knows' what comes after, the portion available now — enjoyment in work — is the wise response. Ki hu helqo — 'for that is his portion.' Helqo (his portion) — the concept introduced at 2:10: the helq that actually belongs to the person, as opposed to the accumulated amal that passes to another. Ki mi yevi'ennu lir'ot bemah sheyih-yeh acharav — 'For who can bring him to see what will be after him?' The future is beyond sight; the present is available. This is not fatalism but realism about the limits of human knowledge.

4.1: Ve-shavthi ani va-er'eh et-kol-ha'ashukot asher na'asim tahat hashemesh — 'I turned and considered all the acts of oppression done under the sun.' The verb shav (to turn/return) marks a new investigation — the Teacher redirects his attention from the times-poem meditation to social observation. Ashukot (H6217, acts of oppression) from ashaq (to oppress/defraud/extort). Ve-hineh dim'at ha'ashukot ve-ein lahem menachem — 'I saw the tears of the oppressed — they had no one to comfort them.' The pathos here is as acute as anywhere in the book: menachem (H5162, comforter) — the absent comforter. Lamentations 1:2 uses the same construction for Jerusalem's weeping with no comforter (ein menachem). The Teacher's observation anticipates Lamentations' grief without offering the resolution that comes only in the eschatological consolation (Isa 40:1: 'Comfort, comfort my people').
4.2: Ve-shabach ani et-ha-metim she-kebar metu — 'So I counted the dead who have already died more fortunate than the living.' Shabach (H7623, to praise/commend/declare fortunate) — the Teacher 'praises' the dead. The word is strong: this is public declaration of the dead's superior condition given the oppression of v.1. Min-ha-chayyim asher hemmah chayyim adenat — 'more fortunate than the living who are still alive.' The comparison is conditional on the context of oppression: death ends the experience of watching oppression without remedy. This is not a death-wish but an honest response to what the Teacher has observed.
4.3: Ve-tov mishneihem et asher-adenat lo hayah — 'But better off than either is the one not yet born.' The better-off formula tov … mi (good/better … than) creates a descending scale: living (worst), dead (better), unborn (best). Asher lo-ra'ah et-hama'aseh hara' sheassoh tahat hashemesh — 'who has not seen the evil that is done under the sun.' Not seeing the evil is the unborn's advantage. The Teacher's bitter logic: the best position is not to have begun. This is the nadir of his social observation — not a permanent conclusion but the honest extreme of what oppression without remedy looks like.
4.4: Ve-ra'iti ani et-kol amal ve-et kol kishron hama'aseh ki hi qin'at ish mere'ehu — 'Then I saw that all toil and every skilled achievement springs from a person's envy of his neighbor.' The word qin'ah (H7068, envy/jealousy/zeal) here in its negative sense: social rivalry as the hidden motor of industriousness. Kishron (H3788, skill/efficiency) appeared at 2:21 for the wise person's abilities. The Teacher now identifies that much of what appears to be virtuous skill (kishron) is actually rivalry-driven (qin'ah). Gam-zeh havel ure'ut ruach — the full verdict: industry driven by envy produces the same futile result as the other experiments.
4.5: Ha-kesil chofek et-yadav ve-okhal et-besaro — 'The fool folds his hands and devours himself.'
